title: Liquid-phase encapsulation of $π$-conjugated dyes in boron nitride nanotubes: Ensemble and single-nanotube optical characterization
authors: Friedrich Schöppler, Lukas Stumpf, Lucas Fuhl, Elena Behr, Charlotte Allard, Christoph Lambert, Richard Martel, Tobias Hertel
abstract: Boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs) provide wide-bandgap, optically transparent one-dimensional hosts for molecular dyes, limiting direct electronic participation of the host. Whether dye@BNNT systems produce bright, well-defined J- or H-aggregates or instead heterogeneous emissive ensembles whose character depends on chain length and local packing remains only partly resolved. We address this question using ensemble extinction, photoluminescence, quantum-yield measurements, and TCSPC-derived radiative and non-radiative rates, together with polarization-resolved single-nanotube microscopy on encapsulated quaterthiophene, sexithiophene, octithiophene, and Nile Red, selected from a ten-dye screening. In the oligothiophene series, confinement modifies spectra and excited-state dynamics in a length-dependent manner, with all three oligothiophenes forming weakly emissive ensembles with suppressed effective radiative rates and 6T showing the strongest redistribution between effective radiative and non-radiative decay. The absence of radiative-rate enhancement or fluorescence-lifetime shortening across the series disfavors bright J-aggregate assignments. Polarization-resolved single-nanotube microscopy reveals strongly polarized emission, but with tube-to-tube and intratube variations, identifying oligothiophene@BNNTs as ordered yet structurally heterogeneous confined ensembles. Nile Red provides a complementary case in which the dominant response is dielectric tuning of a solvatochromic charge-transfer state rather than oligothiophene-like aggregate formation. These findings establish dye-filled BNNTs as optically quiet nanoconfined systems in which molecular ordering, dielectric confinement, and guest-guest coupling can be distinguished through combined ensemble and single-nanotube spectroscopy.
summary: This paper investigates the optical properties of dyes encapsulated inside boron nitride nanotubes using both ensemble and single-nanotube techniques. It demonstrates that the confinement effect is highly dependent on the guest molecule, acting as a dielectric tuner for some dyes and a conformational regulator for others. The findings reveal that these 1D systems form ordered but structurally heterogeneous ensembles.
main_problem: Determining how 1D confinement within boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs) modifies the optical, kinetic, and structural properties of encapsulated pi-conjugated dyes.
main_result: The study finds that BNNT encapsulation induces guest-specific responses, such as dielectric tuning in Nile Red and length-dependent conformational/excitonic modifications in oligothiophenes, resulting in structurally heterogeneous but highly polarized ensembles.
method: Liquid-phase wet-chemical encapsulation followed by ensemble spectroscopy (absorption, PL, PLQY, TCSPC) and polarization-resolved single-nanotube microscopy.
model_or_system: A nanoconfined 1D system consisting of various pi-conjugated dyes (oligothiophenes 4T, 6T, 8T and Nile Red) encapsulated within wide-bandgap, optically transparent boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs).
key_observables: Absorption/extinction spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, PL quantum yield (PLQY), fluorescence lifetimes, radiative and non-radiative decay rates, and degree of linear polarization.
important_parameters: Oligomer length, BNNT inner/outer diameters, dielectric constant of the environment (DMF vs. BNNT interior), and van der Waals cross-sections of the dyes.
main_assumptions: The BNNT host is assumed to be electronically inactive and optically transparent, acting primarily as a dielectric and geometric constraint.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Chemical structures and dye classification; Fig 2: BNNT diameter distributions and comparison to dye dimensions; Fig 3: Normalized absorption and PL spectra for 4T, 6T, 8T, and NR; Fig 5: AFM/PL correlation for 6T; Fig 6: Polarization-resolved PL traces; Fig 7: Spatial contrast maps of single nanotubes; Fig 8: Histograms of polarization degree and angle.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the experimental protocol for dye encapsulation, followed by a systematic study of different dye classes (oligothiophenes vs. Nile Red), an analysis of the kinetic decay rates, and concludes with single-nanotube microscopy to probe structural heterogeneity and orientational order.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of the molecular systems investigated in this study. Top: photographs of representative dye solutions in DMF, illustrating the distinct absorp- tion/emission characteristics of the different dye classes. Bottom: corresponding chemical structures of selected compounds, including α-oligothiophenes (4T, 6T, 8T), squaraine dyes (SQ3, SQA, SQB), phenoxazines (Nile Red, Nile Blue), and rhodamines (Rhodamine B, Rhodamine 6G). This set of dyes spans a range of conjugation lengths and chemical functionalities, providing a basis for comparative encapsulation and optical studies in BNNT hosts.

Figure 2: Correlation between inner and outer diameters of BNNTs determined from HRTEM analysis for outer diameters up to about 7 nm, along with his- tograms of the inner and outer diameter distributions. Data from Allard et al.[23] Dashed sloped lines serve as tentative guides indicating discrete incre- ments of ∼0.34 nm, corresponding to the thickness of a single BN layer. The blue square along the bottom axis indicates the estimated range of vdW dimen- sions of the investigated dye molecules.

Figure 3: Normalized absorption (dashed) and photoluminescence (solid) spectra of representative dyes in solution and after encapsulation in BNNTs (X@BNNT) in DMF at room temperature. The comparison highlights system- atic spectral changes upon encapsulation, including bathochromic shifts and modified vibronic structure, indicative of altered molecular conformation and intermolecular coupling under one-dimensional confinement. While spectral positions and line shapes vary between dyes, encapsulated species consistently exhibit signatures distinct from their solution counterparts. For 4T@BNNT and NR@BNNT, the transmission spectra show dye signatures that are too weak to be reliably separated...

Figure 4: Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) decay curves of oligothiophenes (4T, 6T, 8T) in DMF and after encapsulation in BNNTs (X@BNNT) at room temperature (T = 298 K). The data are shown as nor- malized intensities on a semi-logarithmic scale. Symbols represent the experi- mental data, while solid lines correspond to reconvolution fits.

Figure 5: Correlated AFM and PL images of a representative spin-coated 6T@BNNT sample area on the substrate. a, AFM image showing about a dozen nanotube-like objects (5 µm scale bar). b, PL image of exactly the same area. Most AFM-visible objects also appear as PL-active rods or, for very short nanotubes, as diffraction-limited spots. The comparison provides a qualitative impression that a substantial fraction of the deposited nanotubes is emissive af- ter purification, although it is not intended as a quantitative determination of the filling yield.
